output:
[REASONING CONTEXT]
Amplify: premise-inference mapping; formal validity check; fallacy detection; structural traps; causal ordering check
Suppress: informal reasoning shortcuts; rhetorical validity conflation; mechanism lock; means-ends inversion
[END REASONING CONTEXT]

---

**Step 1 — Decompose the argument into premises, inferences, and conclusion:**

| # | Component | Type |
|---|-----------|------|
| P1 | Worker at $7.25/hr full-time earns $15,080/yr | Premise |
| P2 | $15,080 < poverty line for family of two ($20,440) | Premise |
| P3 | Worker at $20/hr full-time earns $41,600/yr | Premise |
| P4 | $41,600 > poverty line for family of four ($31,200) | Premise |
| I1 | Raising the wage raises the worker's annual income | Inference |
| C | A $20 minimum wage eliminates working poverty | Conclusion |

**Step 2 — Map the inference gap:**

The conclusion doesn't just follow from the arithmetic. It requires an unstated bridging premise. The chain is:

> *Raise wage → same workers still work same full-time hours → income rises as projected → families escape poverty*

**Step 3 — The single most critical unstated assumption:**

> **The wage increase will not reduce employment or hours worked** — i.e., that every current minimum-wage worker will remain employed full-time at the new rate.

**Why this is the critical one:**

The argument's entire mechanism is `wage × hours = income`. If employers respond to a near-tripling of the minimum wage by:
- Laying off low-wage workers
- Cutting hours from full-time to part-time
- Automating positions

...then the workers already in poverty are precisely the ones who lose employment or hours. The arithmetic collapses: a worker earning $0 or 20 hrs/week at $20/hr is *worse off*, not lifted out of poverty. The suppression signal flags this as **mechanism lock** — the argument treats the causal pathway (higher wage → higher income) as fixed, ignoring the feedback loop (higher wage → labor demand response).

**Why other assumptions are secondary:**

The argument also conflates individual worker income with household income (a family of four may need two earners), and ignores price effects — but these are downstream distortions. The employment/hours assumption is load-bearing: if it fails, no poverty reduction occurs at all regardless of the arithmetic.

**Bottom line:** The argument assumes the labor market is perfectly inelastic — that employment and hours are invariant to the wage level. This is the assumption that, if false, makes the conclusion not just overstated but potentially backwards.
